coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen is the prime source of energy with enormous attention in the current research development process as it is safe, clean, eco-friendly, and can be produced from renewable resources through simple catalytic reactions. Scalable production of hydrogen through photocatalysis has been achieved using carbon-modified semiconductors since 2009. In this direction, this review delivers comprehensive understandings into the interface and structural interactions between TiO2 and carbonaceous materials such as carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene, activated carbon, graphitic carbon nitride, carbon quantum dots, etc., and their influences toward improving the hydrogen generation activity of these systems. Besides, recently developed carbonaceous materials such as 3-D graphene, carbon nanohorns, and carbon nanocones have also been discussed on their character in the photocatalytic water splitting procedure. In general, the observed improvements in this carbon-modified TiO2 attributed to the synergetic effects, which offer the active migration of charge carriers and reduced recombination rates in the photocatalyst. Finally, highlighting the future perspectives of the carbonaceous materials in photocatalytic applications are concluded.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogen is the prime source of energy with enormous attention in the current research development process as it is safe, clean, eco-friendly, and can be produced from renewable resources through simple catalytic reactions. Scalable production of hydrogen through photocatalysis has been achieved using carbon-modified semiconductors since 2009. In this direction, this review delivers comprehensive understandings into the interface and structural interactions between TiO2 and carbonaceous materials such as carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene, activated carbon, graphitic carbon nitride, carbon quantum dots, etc., and their influences toward improving the hydrogen generation activity of these systems. Besides, recently developed carbonaceous materials such as 3-D graphene, carbon nanohorns, and carbon nanocones have also been discussed on their character in the photocatalytic water splitting procedure. In general, the observed improvements in this carbon-modified TiO2 attributed to the synergetic effects, which offer the active migration of charge carriers and reduced recombination rates in the photocatalyst. Finally, highlighting the future perspectives of the carbonaceous materials in photocatalytic applications are concluded.

ce:section.ce:para: Fossil fuels, such as oil, coal, and natural gas, still play a crucial role in meeting global energy requirements [1,2]. However, fossil fuels liberate carbon dioxide (CO2) and other greenhouse gases on combustion, which can cause long-term and short-term adverse effects on the environment [3]. To overcome these consequences, the world is always looking for the new energy sources that can replace fossil fuels without any adverse influence on the environment and the efficiency of the fuels [3,4]. Hydrogen (H2) consider as fresh fuel with the maximum potential in standing with mass (120.7 MJ/kg). It has been realized that H2 can be the best alternative to fossil fuels. Globally, around 95% H2 is produced from hydrocarbons, about 4% is generated by electrolysis of water and the remaining 1% from photocatalytic water splitting, thermal splitting, etc., Photocatalytic hydrogen production methods can be classified into two types of energy sources i.e. photo-electrolysis, thermal and photocatalytic water splitting are based on renewable energy sources while steam reforming, gasification methods are based on non-renewable sources [5–8]. Among these, the methods depend upon renewable resources are expected to be economical and environmentally favorable. However, these methods have not become popular due to their very low H2 production efficiency. Interestingly, nowadays photocatalysis process has become a very convenient technique for splitting the water molecules into H2 and O2, as this process shows the better H2 production efficiency with the help of light irradiation [4,9]. The schematic representation of the H2 production methods from different primary energy sources and its significant applications are shown in Fig. 1 . Currently, in countries like Japan, Korea, and the USA are the process of testing the H2 energy towards its real-time applications in transportation and other fields. However, in India, H2 production is still only in the investigation, progress, and demonstration phase.

Fujishima et al., first reported the H2 production over TiO2 semiconductor in 1972 [10]. Consequently, semiconductors like ZnO, CdS, Bi2WO4, etc., have mainly been explored for the H2 generation based on their efficiency of water splitting. Generally, in semiconductors, valance bond (VB) occupied with electrons and conduction band (CB) is empty. When the light irradiated onto the semiconductor with larger than or similar to that of its energy band gap (Eg), then electrons migrate from the VB to CB, and thus holes occupy in VB. Then, the photo-generated electrons and holes are migrated to the surface of semiconductors and involve in the reduction (H2 production) and oxidation (degradation of pollutants) processes, respectively [11,12]. Photocatalysis process involves six steps as schematically shown in Fig. 2 .

Titanium dioxide (TiO2) was the first photocatalyst utilized for the splitting of water owing to its suitable band edge potential (wide bandgap works under ambient conditions), non-toxic, photochemical stability, low cost and abundant in nature [13,14]. Process for the generation of hydrogen through splitting photocatalysis technique is given in the equations (1)–(4), [15].
(1) TiO2 + hυ (>Eg) → TiO2 (e− + h+)
(2) Oxidation: 2H2O + 4 h+ → 4H+ + O2
(3) Reduction: 2H+ + 2e− → H2
(4) Overall Mechanism: H2O + Semiconductor + hυ → H2 + ½ O2

There are two major problems with TiO2, (i) the larger band gap of TiO2 enables absorption of light in UV region only, (ii) the separated electron-hole pair tends to undergo fast recombination [4,16]. To overcome this, TiO2 is modified with the metals, non-metals, noble metals, and other semiconductors [17–20]. The band gap engineering approach in the modification of TiO2 facilitated extended light absorption in the visible spectrum [21]. Among the non-metals, carbon-based nanomaterials are widely used both as sensitizer and co-catalyst. Carbon is one of the widely abundantly available material on the earth, which is largely used in the energy and environmental applications, especially in the form of nanostructures [22]. Carbon nanostructures are CNTs, graphene, CQDs, CNHs, etc., which plays a crucial role in sensors, catalysis, electrocatalysis, energy storage, and gas storage applications thanks to their exclusive properties such as high stability, lightweight, electrical and thermal conductivity [23–26]. Especially, in the PWS process, the carbon-based materials act as a co-catalyst as well as photo-sensitizers, which can facilitate (i) the reduced recombination of charges and (ii) the narrowing of band gap energy of TiO2 [13,27]. In recent years, some novel photocatalysts based on TiO2 have been prepared and their activities were investigated [28–30].

Furthermore, these materials can also rise the adsorption sites and catalytic-active sites, supporting materials through tailoring the properties of carbon materials such as doping effect, interface engineering, and surface chemical functionalization. The schematic representation of carbonaceous materials role and strategies for customizing the features are as shown in Fig. 3 . Recently, some of the authors reviewed with different photocatalyst for hydrogen production [31–33]. Xu et al., studied metal-free carbonaceous materials for the electrocatalysis and photocatalytic water splitting applications [22]. Guancai et al., have examined the graphene-based semiconductor photocatalysts for production H2 under solar light [34]. Shaowen et al., explained the carbon nanostructures in their abilities to enhance the H2 productions [35]. Rowan et al., have given the overall discussion on the carbon-TiO2 nanostructured composites towards their photocatalytic application [23]. In addition, some novel photocatalysts based on g-C3N4 have been prepared and their activities were investigated [36–40]. However, among this literature, the reports on the interfacial effects between the TiO2 nanostructures and carbonaceous materials for the photocatalytic water splitting are considerably rare. It should be noted that in the photocatalysis process, the role of carbon materials is still under debate [41]. However, such study on the interfacial interaction between TiO2 and carbon-based material is essential as electron transfer between the carbon material and semiconductor is critical in the photocatalytic process for the redox reactions to occur on the surface. Generally, a stable interface between the carbon nanostructures and TiO2 plays a key role in enhanced H2 production. In the metal-semiconductor systems, there are three types of interfaces such as Schottky barrier (SB), surface plasmonic resonance (SPR), and the Z-scheme interface that can be formed [42]. These SB, as well as SPR effects, can facilitate the transfer of an electron from the semiconductor to metal as well as metal to semiconductor respectively. In Z-Scheme arrangement, the photoexcited electrons of oxygen developing semiconductors are shifted and united with the holes of hydrogen developing semiconductor [43,44].

In this review, comprehensive insights into the carbon-based materials with TiO2 interface towards improving the H2 production efficiency have been discussed. Significance of recently developed carbon nanostructures such as CQDs, CNHs, and CNCs have also been discussed. Firstly, the TiO2 nanostructures-carbonaceous composite effects towards H2 production have been reviewed and later, the influences of morphology, and interface effects between the carbonaceous-TiO2 photocatalysts have been discussed, and finally, it concluded with the current challenges and future perspectives in the carbonaceous-TiO2 photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pros and cons of TiO2 nanostructures in photocatalysis
ce:section.ce:para:
TiO2 is the most capable semiconductor material for the photocatalytic water splitting (PWS) process [45–47]. Fujishima and Honda have first reported the H2 production on TiO2 semiconductor in 1972 via electrochemical process [10]. Consequently, in the year of 1977, Bard et al., introduced the photocatalysis process with TiO2. Later, TiO2 semiconductors had gained a lot of attraction in the PWS process due to their eco-friendly nature, low-cost and most importantly due to the usage of renewable resources as the source for the production of H2. Despite the active properties of TiO2 for photocatalysis, it did not get popular for many years due to low yield. This low yield of TiO2 is principally owing to the wider band gap, which limited to the absorption of visible & IR light and fast charge carriers recombination process. In addition to these, the backward reactions and few surface active sites also the reasons for the lower efficiency of TiO2. Earlier, TiO2 nanoparticles were widely used for H2 production than the bulk TiO2 as it has a high surface area and more active sites [25,48]. However, TiO2 nanoparticles still possess the following four disadvantages such as.
(i)
Wide bandgap, which limits the effective utilization of sunlight
(ii)
Slow charge transportations by lower interfacial area
(iii)
A single phase of TiO2 induces the fast recombination of photogenerated charge carries
(iv)
Backward reactions

The drawbacks, as mentioned earlier, are graphically represented in Fig. 4 . To overcome the above disadvantages, TiO2 specially designed with different morphologies such as nanotubes, rods, wires, sheets, and hierarchical structures [49]. Graphical representation on the TiO2 nanostructures and its potential properties towards H2 production is showed in Fig. 5 . Through these nanostructures, researchers have achieved the high absorption, efficient charge separation, quantum confinement, and high surface area than the TiO2 nanoparticle [50,51].

Among all the mentioned nanostructures, the 1-D nanostructures such as tubes, rods, and wires have been widely investigated for the PWS under solar light irradiation as they show unidirectional electron flow, high surface area as well as simple synthesis process [52–54]. Lakshmana et al., reported the slightly increased H2 rate with TiO2 nanostructures than the TiO2 (p-25) nanoparticle under UV-LED light [55]. In addition to that, some reports also showed higher H2 production efficiency over TiO2 nanostructures than the TiO2 nanoparticles [56,57]. However, they could not get the remarkable hydrogen production from natural 1-D TiO2 nanostructures as well as hierarchical structures, which is principally owing to their properties like as low specific area, single crystal phase, and flat surface area. The mentioned obstacles are partially surpassed via doping with metal, non-metal, use of co-catalyst, preparation of nanocomposite with metal or metal oxides, and composites between the metal oxide and carbonaceous material. The carbonaceous content (activated carbon, CNTs, g-C3N4, carbon Q-dots), are beneficial in two ways (i) as a photosensitizer and (ii) as co-catalyst. In the review, we have discussed the TiO2-carbonaceous materials for their PWS process with emphasis on how these carbon materials can overcome the mentioned drawbacks in TiO2 thereby enhance the H2 yields in comparison with the bareTiO2 nanostructures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbonaceous materials with TiO2 nanostructures for H2 production

ce:section.ce:para: A.L. Lavoisier first proposed carbon in 1789 from the charcoal. Carbon is one of the most copiously available elements on the world, which can form a vast number of compounds [58]. Carbon has been showed the unique physical, chemical, and thermal properties [59]. Importantly, in the PWS, carbon plays different roles, such as supporting material, co-catalyst, and electron acceptor [60]. These roles of carbon fundamentally improve the surface active sites, visible light absorption, and proficiently transfer the electrons to surface active sites in TiO2 [61,62]. Moreover, which also progress the separation of the photogenerated charge carriers of TiO2, which has gained electron storage capability as well [63].

Gao et al., reported the carbon-doped TiO2 composite for the H2 generation under irradiation of visible light [64]. However, the adding of carbon to TiO2 photocatalyst improved photocatalytic stability [65,66]. Chengwu et al., synthesized the porous carbon doped-TiO2 layers deposited on the shell type TiC (TiC/TiO2-C) through thermal growth method [67]. This TiC/TiO2-C core-shell exhibited the improved H2 generation results than the pristine TiO2 (P-25) due to core-shell structure that shifted the band edge of TiO2, and which is also beneficial to retard the electron-hole recombination.

The combination of carbon-TiO2 improves the surface reactive sites. The carbon-TiO2 catalyst show enhanced H2 production, but not remarkable. As the addition of carbon to TiO2 may not form strong chemical interface by using chemical methods because pristine carbon is hydrophobic in nature. In addition, slightly higher content of carbon in the composite system lead to screening effect and hence reduced efficiency of the composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence activated carbon (AC) with TiO2 nanostructures
ce:section.ce:para: AC is also one of the carbon family, and it has the amorphous with porosity all the pore ranges, i.e., meso (1–25 nm), macro (>25 nm) and micro (<1 nm) pore ranges [68]. Along with that, AC has a high surface area, high adsorption capacity, chemically stable in structure, and quickly forms a composite with TiO2 [69,70]. Based on these properties, researchers realized its importance in the photocatalysis. In the PWS process, AC can also play the carbon role and that carbon role discussed in section Carbon-TiO2 nanostructure photocatalyst. In addition to that, it also plays supporting material and transport channel for the charge carriers [71–73]. Further, AC also improves the active sites and a stable interface among the AC and TiO2 [74–76]. Based on the literature, removal of hazardous dyes [71,75,77–80]. But, minimal reports are available on the photocatalytic water splitting [47,81].

Hakamizadeh et al. [82], synthesized the Pt/activated carbon/TiO2 photocatalyst through sol-gel and photodecomposition methods. PWS tests for the prepared catalyst were performed under mercury light irradiation, and methanol used as the hole scavenger. The Pt/TiO2/AC catalyst showed around 75 times higher H2 rate than the pristine TiO2. These improvements could be ascribed to the Pt, which increased the surface active sites and also acted as a co-catalyst leading to hindered the recombination. Here, AC played the role of an electron acceptor that effectively transferred electron between TiO2 and Pt [83].

The AC-TiO2 nanocomposite for H2 production reports are very scarce. Mostly, AC -TiO2 showed higher degradation efficiencies under UV and visible light irradiations [71,84]. In the photocatalytic H2 production, AC can play the role of co-catalyst and hinder the recombination rate of charge carriers. The AC-TiO2 nanocomposites has not improved the light absorption region towards visible light. However, Pt added AC-TiO2 ternary photocatalyst had showed absorption in the visible region. Due to the porous structure of AC, TiO2 nanoparticles can interact the AC via pores, thereby forming the strong interface between the AC and TiO2. If strong chemical interactions can be formed between them, that can lead to improved yield in photocatalytic hydrogen production.

CQDs are one of the freshly established carbon nanostructure [26]. CQDs are discovered by Xu et al., in 2004 while carrying out single-walled carbon nanotubes purification process [94]. This new class of carbon materials holds massive attraction due to their exotic characters like environmental friendliness, good conductivity, low toxicity, high stability, optical properties as well as simple synthesis routes [85–87]. Recently CQDs with TiO2 photocatalyst reported for the H2 production via PWS [26,35]. In the photocatalysis, CQDs performed as both co-catalyst and photosensitizer. Also, CQDs play the role of an electron acceptor as well as a channel of charge carrier transporter [88,89].

CQDs/TiO2 composite reported for photocatalytic H2 evolution by Zheng et al. [85]. CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 photocatalyst prepared through alkali-assisted electrochemical and hydrothermal methods, respectively. The H2 production activity tests carried under the UV light with methanol used as for hole scavenger. Among that the developed catalysts, the CQDs-Mixed TiO2 photocatalyst reported for H2 development under UV-light irradiation [90]. The Pt added CQDs/Mixed Crystal TiO2 catalyst showed 280 μmol h−1 H2 evolutions. The CQDs/TiO2 photocatalyst and H2 evolution mechanisms are schematically represented in Fig. 6 .

The CQDs serve as a reservoir for electrons to improve the separation of the electron-hole pairs of TiO2 [91]. Moreover, the Π conjugated CQDs may act as a photosensitizer, like organic dyes, and the photoexcited electrons transfer from the CQDs to TiO2. The CQDs and TiO2 composite generate H2 with pure water under UV light irradiation without sacrificial agent with very low yield (0.5 μmol/h) [91]. However, synthesis of CQDs by green methods using orange, lemon juice etc., can be cost effective and environmental friendly compared to other carbon nanostructures. Therefore, CQDs can provide new promises in a photocatalysis both as Co-Catalyst and Sensitizer [92].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon nanotube (CNTs) - TiO2 nanostructures for H2 production
ce:section.ce:para: The rolling up graphene sheets construct CNTs with tubular morphology [93,94]. Sumio Iijima discovers CNTs in 1991 [95]. CNTs possess high thermal conductivity, electron emission, aspect ratio, elasticity, electrical conductivity, as well as surface area [96–98]. From the last decade, CNTs used for the photocatalytic applications as the chiral indices of CNTs exhibit both metallic and semiconducting properties [99,100]. CNTs can effectively resolve the problems faced by the semiconductors in photocatalysis. Therefore, due to the supporting nature of CNTs such as their role as a co-catalyst, photosensitizer, band gap narrower of TiO2, electron acceptor, and transport channel abilities, CNTs have been effectively suppressed the TiO2 drawbacks and enhanced H2 efficiencies. According to the reported researches, CNTs can show the all mentioned roles in the photocatalysis, but one position such as photosensitizer is challenging to understand because of the factor that the CNTs are conductors so that they do not act as photosensitizer [101,102]. However, some reports claim that the CNTs act as co-catalyst as well as photosensitizer [103,104]. However, this concept is still under debate, particularly in the process of PWS [101,102]. Recently, CNTs are creating new possibilities in chemistry and material science as well as which is offering to create efficient photocatalyst materials with high stability [93,105,106].

MWCNT/Pd–TiO2 composite hierarchical catalysts have been designed and demonstrated for the improved H2 production from the biomass-derived alcohols [41]. The following three steps have synthesized this particular photocatalyst. (i) MWCNTs and Pd nanoparticles (Pd NPs) were individually functionalized with benzoic and mercapto-uneconomic acids, respectively. (ii) Titanium butoxide as titanium precursor is added to the acid treated Pd through self-assembly process. (iii) Finally, the prepared Pd/TiO2 catalyst added to the FMWCNTs through hydrolysis to obtain the final hierarchical hybrid structure. The schematic representation of the synthesis procedure for bond formations between the Pd/CNT-TiO2 photocatalyst is shown in Fig. 7 (a–c). The synthesized photocatalyst H2 production activity tested under UV light, where it showed the H2 production of 25 mmol g−1 h−1. This high hydrogen rate due to the formed electronic junction between the MWCNT-TiO2, where this junction supported the effective charge transfer between the two components involved [107–109]. Moreover, CNT acted as a co-catalyst and effectively transferred the electrons between the TiO2 and Pd. The possible photocatalytic H2 production mechanism is shown in Fig. 7(d).

The hydrothermally prepared TiO2 nanotubes (TNTs)-MWCNTs nanocomposite was used for the H2 generation under light irradiation of UV-vis [110]. TNTs showed better H2 production than the pristine TiO2, and further, the TNTs mediated H2 efficiency was upgraded by the adding of CNTs [as shown in Fig. 8 (a)]. Here the CNTs act as supporting material and as a co-catalyst, which effectively hindered the electron-hole recombination. The TNT morphology on MWCNTs as exposed in Fig. 8(b) [111]. Mamatha et al., also reported the tubular structure-mediated properties of CNT-TNTs photocatalysts for the improved H2 production [27]. TNTs have better optical properties with the uni-directional flow of charge carriers and higher reactive sites than the TiO2 nanoparticles, which lead to the enhanced photocatalytic H2 efficacy [112]. The recently published reports on the CNT-TiO2 nanostructure photocatalyst for H2 production are given in Table 1 . In addition to that, preparation methods, co-catalyst, light sources, and sacrificial agents, as well as H2 production rates and quantum efficiencies, are also included in Table 1.

Hydrogen production efficiency can be improved by the addition of CNTs to the titanate composites due to the uni-directional flow of charge carriers and higher reactive sites. Also, CNTs act as supporting material as a co-catalyst, which effectively hinder the electron-hole recombination in the system. Also, the electron junction between the CNT-TiO2 could support the effective charge carrier transfer to the surface. From literature, it is clear that CNTs acts as co-catalyst, but question remains unclear about its role as sensitizer. Eventhough reports on CNTs function as sensitizer exist, but origin of the photoconductivity is ambiguious. The SWCNTs are more favorable for charge transfer as compared to the MWCNTs, due to the mid-gap bands, causing the possible multi-electron–hole pair generation by absorption of single high energy photons in the UV region across these multiple low energy bandgaps [113–115].

Graphene oxide was introduced by B.C Brodie in 1859 [116]. After such an invention, a lot of theoretical and experimental work has been done on the graphite and developed many new strategies. Recently, Sp2 bonded graphene has been discovered by K S Novesly and his group in 2004 [117]. Graphene is the latest 2-D carbon materials and a member of conjugated carbon materials [118]. In recent years, graphene shows the tremendous interest in the research field and especially in the energy conversion application based on their unique things such as optical transmittance (∼97.7%), high exceptional conductivity (106 sm−1), surface area (2630 m2 g−1), charge carriers mobility (200,000 cm2 v−1s−1), highly stable and thermal conductivity (∼5000 W m−1k−1) [119,120]. Based on these properties, graphene -TiO2 photocatalysts have been widely reported for H2 production as compared with the other carbon (CNTs, CQDs, carbon, AC) materials. As mentioned earlier, the role of CNTs for PWS is similar to the role of graphene. However, the part of CNTs as a photosensitizer is still under debate, while it is confirmed that the graphene can act as a photosensitizer [121]. Based on these properties, graphene-TiO2 photocatalyst widely reported towards the H2 production [35,120,122,123].

Dejian et al., reported the Pt loaded nano spherical RGO-TiO2 (NS-RGO-TiO2) photocatalyst for H2 production [124]. Pt/NS-RGO-TiO2 ternary catalyst showed the highest H2 efficiency than the RGO-TiO2 binary catalyst due to Pt that acts as co-catalyst and effectively hindered the recombination rate of charge carriers. Along with that, the NS-RGO/TiO2 catalyst showed the highest H2 rate than the pristine TiO2 due to NS-RGO acted as electron collector, transporter and also improved the surface active sites. The H2 production and photocatalytic water splitting mechanism showed in Fig. 9 . Interestingly, Au added Graphene-TiO2 nanocomposite also reported for H2 production under solar light irradiation [125]. This optimized composite showed the highest H2 than the pristine TiO2. This high H2 of Au/TiO2 is attributed to Au nanoparticles broadening a visible light response from SPR effect and graphene facilitating the space separation of photogenerated charge carriers [126].

The SPR process for electron migration and the energy band structure of Au–TiO2–graphene catalyst process as displayed in Fig. 10 . Also, the Ag added graphene-TiO2 photocatalyst reported for the improved H2 production as Ag constrained the recombination of charge carriers [132].

Recently published reports on the Graphene-TiO2 nanostructured photocatalysts for H2 production as showed in Table 2 . In addition to that, preparation methods, co-catalyst, light sources, and sacrificial agents, as well as H2 production rates and quantum efficiencies, are also included in Table 2.

Graphene widely reported for the H2 production because of unique properties which offer the more advantages in photocatalysis than the other carbon materials. In photocatalysis, graphene layers effectively stack the excited electrons from the semiconductors while reducing the recombination rate effectively [133]. In addition, graphene added TiO2 photocatalysts showed the absorption in visible range. Besides, graphene also facilitate the space separation of photogenerated charge carriers [104,134]. The incorporation of GO with ultrafine TiO2 particles made an appropriate junction to improve the H2 production efficiency. Thus, photogenerated CB electrons in TiO2 could quickly transfer to GO, due to the excellent interface between the TiO2 and GO layer [135–137].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recently developed carbonaceous nanostructures with TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the new carbonaceous materials such as carbon nanocones (CNCs), carbon nanohorns (CNHs) and 3-D graphene used for the enhancement of H2 production efficiency of TiO2 semiconductors [103,126]. These carbon materials also act as co-catalyst, photosensitizer, and as electron sinks, which facilitate the hindrance of the recombination of charge carriers and accelerate the fast charge migration to the surface [138,139].

CNCs represents an identical class of carbon materials with cone-like structure showed fascinating outcomes in photocatalysis and H2 storage [108]. CNCs may open new perspectives as auxiliary materials for a metal catalyst. The lack of their unique shape and group processes, as seen in CNTs, are easily the result of manipulation and improved H2 productivity [138]. CNCs/TiO2 photocatalyst with Pd co-catalyst reported for H2 production [108]. This photocatalyst showed the outstanding H2 generation of 5 mmol g−1 h−1 after 20 h irradiation and quantum efficiency (QE) of 12.3%. This highest H2 production is attributed to the efficient electron sink effect of CNCs, which resulted in reduced the charge carrier recombination.

Carbon nanohorns (CNHs) are a tubular structure made by the graphene nanosheets [140]. CNHs have high specific surface areas, dispersion ability, and conductivity [139]. However, CNHs also played electron acceptor role from the TiO2 semiconductor surface. On that basis, CNHs have effectively hindered the recombination rate of charge carriers, and thereby, the photocatalytic activity can be enhanced [141]. Along with that, CNHs also acts as a photosensitizer, which can also provide the electron for the redox reaction. Recently, the CNHs with TiO2 photocatalyst reported for H2 production with pre-oxidation versus post oxidation effects under natural light irradiation [142]. Among all synthesized photocatalyst, the pre-oxidized CNHs-TiO2 nanohybrid due to the formation of nano windows showed highest H2 efficiency [143]. This windows opening process and H2 production mechanism are showed in Fig. 11 .

Recently, the core-shell morphological photocatalyst is a most audible word in the photocatalysis as because these structures give the high surface area and surface active sites, which can also show the strong interface interactions between the core and shell [149,150]. Along with that, the core-shell photocatalyst effectively hindered the electron-hole recombination as well as a backward reaction [151]. Owing to these properties, core-shell structures generally improve the overall H2 production efficiency. Moreover, in the core-shell PWS process, the shell protects the core from the reaction solution and this shell can transfer the light into the core then core can excite the electrons to CB from the VB and excited CB electrons tapped by the shell [152–154] thereby it improves the photocatalytic rate of the catalyst. The schematic representation of normal photocatalyst and core-shell photocatalyst water splitting mechanism showed in Fig. 12 . Normal photocatalyst, suffers from rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pair, but core-shell type of photocatalyst effectively diminishes the recombination rate of charge carriers [152,153].

ce:section.ce:section-title: TiO2-carbonaceous core-shell photocatalyst for H2 production
ce:section.ce:para: Core-shell photocatalyst showed positive results with different material like CdS/ZnS, CdS/ZnO, CdS/NiO [151,155–157]. Apart from that, recently, TiO2-carbonaceous core-shell photocatalyst also reported for the H2 production [134].

Nanographene oxide (NGO) shell with TiO2 core photocatalyst reported for both photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical applications [134]. TiO2-NGO core-shell photocatalyst prepared through two-step oxidation processes and H2 production test done under irradiation of UV-visible light. Core-shell photocatalyst showed high H2 production as it can proficiently hinder the recombination and transfer the electron to the surface of TiO2 and especially core-shell structure limits the backward reaction. TiO2-NGO core-shell photocatalyst preparation and H2 production process are schematically shown in Fig. 13 . Moreover, Ziyan et al., synthesized CNT/TiO2 core-shell nanocomposite by a sol-gel process which used for the H2 generation and photo electrocatalytic applications [101].

However, TiO2–carbon core-shell photocatalysts reported for the removal of hazards dyes via photocatalysis [158,159]. Based on literature survey, TiO2 - carbonaceous core-shell structure for the H2 production reports are very rare compared to the other photocatalyst such as CdS/ZnS, CdS/ZnO, CdS/NiO, CdSe/CdS [155–157]. Also, carbon nanostructures have tubular, sheets, dots and cone morphologies, and therefore their exact coating on TiO2 core with ultra-thin shell synthesis process is delicate.

The interfaces occur between the substances, phases, dissimilar materials, and different objects as well as also deals the physical properties of chemical substances [160,161]. In the photocatalysis process, three different types of interfaces, such as energy band, interface, and surface constructions, play a pivotal role in enhancing photocatalytic abilities [161–163]. Aforementioned interfaces make the improvements of photon-harvesting ability thus easily transfer the electrons from VB to CB, generate the charges, limit the recombination rate of charge, prolong their life time. Thus allows the more electrons and holes to the surface of semiconductor for redox reactions and improves the reactive abilities on the surface thus effectively participate in the reactions, respectively [164–168]. In this, interface and surface constructions effects for the photocatalytic H2 production are discussed. According to theoretical study as well as reported literature, the basic possibilities to construct interfaces are four different types of surface and interfaces such as Schottky Barrier (SB), Plasmonic/ohmic junction, heterojunctions and Z-scheme interfaces between carbonaceous and TiO2 materials [162,169]. In addition to that, in the interface and surface constructions, parameters such as interfacial composition, surface composition, defects, areas, pores, vacancies, facets, phases, electronic coupling and surface states also influenced to the efficacy of hydrogen production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Basic conditions for making different interfaces and electrons flow process between carbonaceous and TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, we discuss the basic requirements of the materials for the formation of different interfaces constructions between the carbonaceous and TiO2. Moreover, the resultant interface charge carriers transfer processes also discussed.

Schottky barrier (SB) formed when the work function of the metal (Φm) is greater than the semiconductor work function (Φsem) [Φm > Φsem]. In this, metals fermi level is lesser than the semiconductors. So the flow of electron process occur from the semiconductor to metal. These electron flows continue until the Fermi levels of both semiconductor and metal reach an equilibrium point and then form the potential barrier between them. Besides, the bands bend up in the path of the electric field [42]. This means the energy bands bend up going from an n-type semiconductor to metal. This bending expression can be written as,
ΦB = Φm – Xsem
where ΦB and Φm are the schottky barrier and metal work function respectively. Metal work function generally distinguished as the essential potential to trap an electron from the metal Fermi level to vacuum. Also, Xsem is the electron affinity which is defined as the distance between the minimum CB to the vacuum level. Generally, the SB acts as the electron acceptor from the semiconductor, resulting the prevention of the recombination rate of charge carriers [42,170]. The SB formation process of semiconductor and metal pictorial representation is shown in Fig. 14 . Based on the work function of carbon materials (4.81 eV) [CNS work functions depends on their structure, size as well as area but most of the cases possible higher work function than the anatase TiO2 semiconductor] there is a possibility of the establishment of SB interface among carbonaceous nanostructures and TiO2 [93,171]. The photogenerated electrons flow between semiconductor and metal with SB junction schematic representation are displayed in Fig. 15 (a). The detailed discussion about the electron transfer process with the SB junction explained in the next section with carbon-TiO2 photocatalyst.

Ohmic junction formed when the semiconductor work function (Φsem) is higher than the metal work function (Φm) [Φm ˂ Φsem]. In the ohmic intersection, metals Fermi level is higher than the semiconductors Fermi level, thereby the electron flow process occurs from the metal to semiconductor. This ohmic junction mainly occurs between the noble metal like Pt, Au, and non-noble metal like Cu and TiO2 [121,146,172,173].

In Z-Scheme arrangement, the photoexcited electrons of oxygen developing semiconductors are shifted and united with the holes of hydrogen developing semiconductor. However, the photo-excited electrons are left in the CB of hydrogen developing semiconductors which tend to diminish H+, and the holes remained in the VB of oxygen-evolving semiconductors are used to oxidize H2O [43,44]. When the light irradiated on the semiconductor, electrons leave from VB of semiconductor A and occupy the CB of the same semiconductor. The excited CB electrons in semiconductor A then transfer to semiconductor B of VB and combine with holes. Further, the electrons in the B semiconductor CB are used to reduce the H2O as H2, and holes in a semiconductor A of CB are used to oxidize H2O to O2. The charge transfer process of the Z-scheme interface schematic representation is shown in Fig. 15(b).
The heterojunction interface mainly occurs between two layers or dissimilar crystalline semiconductor materials or two unequal energy bandgaps of semiconductors.

In type I heterojunction, holes and electrons are accumulated in semiconductor B since semiconductor A has lower negative edge potential than the semiconductor B. The electron transfer effect during the formation of type I heterojunction is schematically represented in Fig. 15(c).

Type II heterojunction is similar to the Z-scheme interface. In this, the created VB holes of semiconductor A transfer to the VB of semiconductor B and CB electrons of B transfer to the CB of A. The electron transfer effect during the formation of type II heterojunction is schematically represented in Fig. 15(d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible ways to make different interfaces between the 0D CNS-TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), Graphene quantum dots (GQD)s, etc., are widely used zero dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterials for the photocatalytic applications [26,35]. Previously, carbon coated stabilized Ti3+ doped TiO2 photocatalyst reported for the H2 production under visible light irradiation. These carbon coated TiO2 photocatalyst extend the light absorption region towards the visible area [64]. In addition to that, carbon doped TiC@C-TiO2 core-shell photocatalyst also reported for the H2 production under the visible light irradiation [67]. Reported literature showed the formation of Ti-O-C chemical bonding between the carbon and TiO2, which resulted in effectively transfer and hindered the recombination rate of charge carriers. However, based on the theoretical study, SB construction formation is possible between the carbon-TiO2 since the carbon nanomaterials have higher work function than anatase TiO2 [113]. The formed SB construction between the carbon and TiO2 facilitate the suppressing the recombination of the generated electron-hole pair which then allows the electron-holes to carry out redox reactions [170]. In the photocatalytic H2 production mechanism of carbon and TiO2 photocatalyst with SB junction, light irradiated on the semiconductor with photon energy greater than or equal to the energy bandgap, electrons leave the VB and occupy the CB. The occupied CB electrons can migrate to the surface of the semiconductor with the help of carbon and SB junction. Finally, excited electrons involved in the reduction reaction then produce the hydrogen. In addition to that, carbon acts as co-catalyst or electron sink. The electron transformation process and photocatalytic water splitting mechanism between the carbon and TiO2 with SB junction pictorial representation are displayed in Fig. 16 .

In the case of CQDs and TiO2 photocatalyst, also reported the Ti-O-C chemical bond construction between them [85,90]. CQDs acts as co-catalyst, sensitizer, and electron sinks in the photocatalysis process. In addition to that, Yunqi et al., mentioned the formation of heterojunction between the two phases of TiO2, but they did not give how the heterojunction formed between the two phases [90]. According to the heterojunction construction theoretical study bases, its construction is possible from the two dissimilar semiconductors. In addition to that, they have Ti3+ state formation on the surface and thus surface oxygen vacancies reduce the energy band of the semiconductor as well as enhance the efficacy of H2 generation.

Based on the literature, there is a possibility of formation of strong chemical bonding such as Ti-O-C between the CQDs and TiO2 which results in suppression of electron-hole recombination and effectively migrate CB electrons to TiO2 surface. The interface interactions between CQDs and TiO2 were investigated by the X-PS spectra. The resultant X-PS spectra of CQDs-TiO2 photocatalyst is represented in Fig. 17 . The CQDs have smaller energy bandgap, but no one reported the exact bandgap and CB and VB edge potential of CQDs. Also, Energy bandgap of nano materials depend on the size. So, there is difficulty in the assumption of the mentioned interfaces between the CQDs and TiO2.

The GQDs are also one of the most promising 0D carbon nanostructures materials which are widely used for the enhanced photocatalytic efficiency [174,175]. According to the interface construction, most of the previous reports explained the cause of enhanced photocatalytic efficiency due to the strong interfacial interaction formed between the GQDs and TiO2, resulting in Ti-O-C chemical bonding. Furthermore, GQDs also act as solid-state electron transfer as well as the sensitizer in the photocatalysis process [176]. The GQDs photocatalytic H2 production with TiO2 semiconductor is shown in Fig. 18 .

From Fig. 18, GQDs have small energy band gap and also have negative CB edge potential and positive VB potential which are suitable for reduction and oxidation reactions respectively. On that basis, GQDs and TiO2 can form heterojunction construction because of the interface formed between the two layers or regions is dissimilar kinds. In the heterojunction construction, when light irradiated on the GQDs electrons excited from VB to CB. These excited electrons transfer to the CB of TiO2 and then involve in the redox reaction. Simultaneously, TiO2 can move holes to the GQDs VB, which further reduces the recombination of the electron-hole pair. Heterojunction formed GQDs and TiO2 electron transformation process for reduction reactions are shown in Fig. 19 . The heterojunctions beneficial to the improvement of photon absorption strength, hinder the recombination rate of charge carriers prolong time and a transfer rate of charge carriers to the surface of semiconductor [169,177]. One of the essential strategies of GQDs is the energy band gap, and CB and VB edge potentials are depended on their size and addition of impurities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible ways to make different interfaces between the 1D CNS-TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: The MWCNTs, SWCNTs are widely used 1D carbon nanostructures in the photocatalysis process [114,130]. However, the interface construction process of TiO2 with CNTs is still in understanding process because of reported literature on these did not give clear information about the interface construction. Most of the research said the strong Ti-O-C chemical bonding and/or metal-semiconductor junction interface formed between them [100,103,127]. These intimated interactions enhance the photo-excited CB electron to the surface of TiO2 and high surface area of CNS effectively improve the surface reaction sites, thus enhance hydrogen production efficiency between the CNT-TiO2. However, SB interface is possible between the CNTs and TiO2 as CNTs have higher work function than the TiO2 [169]. That higher Fermi level of semiconductor cause electrons flows from the semiconductor to metal [93,111,178]. One more important strategy is CNTs work function is mainly depend on the preparation process. However, If SB formed between the CNTs -TiO2, the electron transformation process is similar to the carbon and TiO2 photocatalyst. Generally, schottky barrier is designed for shifting electrons and holes to surfaces and reduced the recombination rate of charge carriers. In this when two components get into contact, the work function of the component for accepting electrons should be higher than that of the component on the other side of interface. Nevertheless, upon photoexcitation, the orders for charge transfer would depend on specific charge kinetic models. The SB construction developed carbon-TiO2 photocatalytic water splitting mechanism schematic representation is displayed in Fig. 16.

TiO2 nanoparticles decorated on the carbon nanotube (TNP/CNTs) photocatalysts studied for H2 production via water splitting [113]. Pt loaded TNP/CNTs catalyst showed the highest H2 production due to the Schottky barrier formation between the metal-TiO2. CNTs can trap the photoexcited electron and create the local potential difference in TiO2 phase, resulting in reduced lifetime of charge carriers recombination. Simultaneously, highly efficient carbon doped CNT/TNT photocatalyst reported for the H2 production under solar simulator [50]. The synthesized catalyst presented H2 generation rate is 37.6 mmol g−1 h−1, which could be due to the SB formation between the CNT-TNT (CTCNT). The density of states (DOS) formed in CTCNT catalyst, band gap narrowing, and electron transfer between the Pt/CTCNT catalysts are shown in Fig. 20 (a–b). Electronic transitions from the VB to the localized states can reduce by 0.88 eV, which principals to the reduction in band gap of the CTCNT photocatalyst. The highest quantum efficiency of 95% has been achieved under UV light irradiation, which is high ever reported on the TiO2 based photocatalyst [113].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible ways to make different interfaces of 2D CNS-TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene and g-C3N4 are the most promising 2D Carbon materials which are widely used in the photocatalytic application with a combination of TiO2 [13,179–181]. For coming to the concept of interface effects between graphene and TiO2, most of the studies explained graphene-TiO2 with noble metals for hydrogen production [121,182]. Also, electronic transfer occurs with surface plasmonic resonance effect (SPR) because of the plasmonic nature of noble metals [121,183]. Previous reports are discussed the graphene role only in the photocatalysts process such as its acts as co-catalyst and graphene have a two-dimensional Π-conjugate structure that helps the excellent electron acceptor with superior conductivity. Xiao et al., reported the Cu/graphene-TiO2 photocatalyst with ohmic interface construction between the Cu and TiO2 as well discussed aforementioned that graphene acts as co-catalyst and electron transport channel between the Cu and TiO2 [146]. However, Singh et al., reported the ohmic interaction between the Au/TiO2 and SPR effect as well as graphene acts as a co-catalyst [121]. As per the literature survey bases, minimal reports are explained the interface effects between the graphene and TiO2 related to the photocatalysis process. Generally, the graphene Fermi level is lower than the CB of most photocatalysts, thus the electrons in the CB of the photocatalysts can be easily transferred from the photocatalysts to the graphene, which enhances the separation of the electrons and holes. According to the role of graphene and which properties bases there is possible to the formation of graphene-semiconductor heterojunction interface construction among graphene and TiO2.

The intimate interaction between modified graphene - TiO2 nanosheets photocatalyst with inter-plane interface junction for H2 production reported by quanjun et al., [135]. In this, the CB potential level is −0.24 V anatase TiO2 and graphene/graphene− the potential is −0.08 V vs SHE at pH = 0. After mentioned potential level bases, graphene/graphene− have a lower potential than the TiO2 and electron transform from CB of TiO2 to graphene. The electron transfer process and photocatalytic hydrogen production mechanism schematic representation as displayed in Fig. 21 . The photocatalytic H2 production efficiency greatly enhanced the in the graphene-TiO2 photocatalyst. The optimum catalyst showed the 41 times higher H2 production efficiency than the alone TiO2 nanosheets with 3.1% quantum efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: g-C3N4 and TiO2 interface influence for H2 production
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, g-C3N4 has become popular in the area of photocatalysis within a short time. Which could be possible due to its energy band gap of 2.7 eV that as directly attracted for the photocatalytic applications [22,35,184]. Notably, g-C3N4 is an eco-friendly, earth-abundant, and visible active material [185,186]. However, other carbonaceous materials could not be utilized directly to the photocatalysis because lower Eg (<1 eV), which essentially means that they can act as conductors, while the g-C3N4 has suitable band gap energy and therefore it can be directly utilized for the H2 production. Energy band gap differences, as well as CB and VB edge potentials of TiO2 and g-C3N4 schematic representation, are displayed in Fig. 22 . Recently, Wang et al., reviewed on the metal free g-C3N4 semiconductor for H2 and explained beneficial interfacial properties with TiO2 [29]. In this scenario, according to the earlier reports, it is possible to have a heterojunction between g-C3N4 and TiO2. For instance, Shen et al., reported the TiO2 nanobelts/g-C3N4 nanosheets for photocatalytic application with heterojunction interface [187]. Moreover, Chen et al., reported the Boron doped-TiO2/g-C3N4 heterojunction for the hydrogen production in visible light driven with {001} facets [188]. Yu et al., reviewed on the surface engineering effects for the enhanced H2 evolution rate of g-C3N4 [189]. Jiaguo et al., reported the g-C3N4-TiO2 photocatalyst for formaldehyde decomposition on air with direct Z-scheme interface [190]. In the PWS process, most of them constructed the heterojunction interface between TiO2 and g-C3N4 [191–195].

According to the bases of the previous report designed g-C3N4-TiO2 photocatalyst H2 production mechanism with the heterojunction interface and electron transfer process for the H2 production process, a schematic representation is displayed in Fig. 25 . The band gap of g-C3N4 is 2.7 eV HOMO, and LUMO potentials of g-C3N4 are 1.3 and 1.4 eV, respectively [197]. As we know, the potential edges of TiO2 are −0.46 and 2.70 eV, respectively [198]. However, redox reaction potentials are −0.41 eV and +0.82 eV. Since g-C3N4 has required reduction and oxidation potentials, it can involve in the redox-reactions along with TiO2. The g-C3N4 additional advantage is that it can directly absorb the visible light due its small energy band gap than the TiO2. The photon energy absorbs the g-C3N4 semiconductor then creates the electron-hole pair. According to the potential edge bases of g-C3N4 excited electron migrate to TiO2 from g-C3N4 CB to carry out reduction reactions on the surface of the TiO2 photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible ways make the different interface between the 3D CNS-TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: The 3-D graphene nanostructures have carried the graphene properties and can also provide super-molecular interaction such as H2 bonding, π-π stacking, and electrostatic interaction [199,200]. Importantly, 3-D graphene containing the interconnected pores prevents the restacking of graphene sheets that eventually improve the high density of the pores and high specific surface area of the system. Owing to these properties, the 3-D graphene structures are widely used in high potential applications such as energy storage, supercapacitors, gas sensors, and catalysis [201]. Recently, 3-D graphene-hydrogels (NGH) that connected with the TiO2 nanorods (TNRs) and Au nanoparticles photocatalysts have been reported for the H2 production under UV-Visible as well as visible light irradiations [126]. In this, Au/RGO-TiO2 photocatalyst showed the absorption in the visible spectrum its means which effectively utilizate the solar spectrum compare to pristine TiO2. However, graphene nanogel acted as transport channel between the Au and TNRs. In this, they showed strong chemical bonding between the NGO and TiO2. Synthesis process of NGH-TNRs/Au photocatalyst, H2 production details, and PWS mechanism schematic representation are showed in Fig. 26 .

The construction of the SB and heterojunctions are possible by hydrothermal, and sonochemical methods. In addition, chemical vapor deposition (CVD) is one of the active method to construct mentioned interfaces. Usually in the CVD process, mixed gas molecules are injected into a reaction chamber which is set at a certain temperature. When the mixed gases come into contact with the substrate within the reaction chamber, a reaction occurs that creates a material film on the substrate surface. However, the different photocatalyst interfaces and synthesis methods are shown in Table 3 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages and disadvantages of the modified TiO2 with carbonaceous materials

ce:section.ce:para: Aforementioned literature and resultant bases, modified TiO2 showed the enormous advantages in photocatalysis such as the absorption region of TiO2 extended towards to the visible region, effectively hinder the recombination rate charge carriers and improve the surface active sites. In addition, photocatalyst H2 production efficacies are enhanced than the pristine TiO2.

Despite the positive results in photocatalysis from modified TiO2, there are still some disadvantages. The disadvantages are.
1.
Modified TiO2 also fails to utilize the entire solar spectrum because with the addition of dopants it can only be enhanced absorption towards visible region but not to the IR region.
2.
If dopants are added at a higher content, the hydrogen production capacity of the dopants added will be lower than the pristine TiO2 because screening effects are started.
3.
In case, strong chemical bond is not formed between TiO2 and dopant, agglomeration will be started in the photocatalysis process then automatically reduce the efficiency of H2.
4.
Moreover, the modification process requires some harmful and expansive chemicals, and some processes are taken a long time to modify TiO2.

In the process of photocatalytic H2 production, it is difficult to determine the best system for H2 production. This is because the synthesis process, synthesis methods, interfaces, using precursors, calcination temperature, time, catalyst phases and photocatalytic experiments using quartz reactors, catalyst weight, sacrificial agents, light sources and gas analysis methods have an impact on the resulting H2 production. However, here we have represented the some of the different photocatalysts for H2 production in Table 4 .

From the Table 4, NiO/TiO2 catalyst showed the highest H2 production which is 2,04,840 μmol h−1 g−1. The conclusion for the best photocatalyst from the Table 4, Cu doped photocatalyst under solar light irradiation with glycerol aqueous solution steadily showed the higher H2 production efficiencies.

Here, given the some key points to say best photocatalyst for hydrogen production.
1.
Synthesized photocatalyst must be low-cost and eco-friendly nature.
2.
Photocatalyst experiments should be done with the renewable resources like water and natural sunlight. A more advanced method is to use seawater. In addition, bio-degradable wastes such as crude glycerol, glycerol etc., used as the sacrificial agents could be a better option.
3.
It is wise to synthesize without expensive and harmful solvents as well as in very short synthesis duration.
4.
Finally, one sentence about the best photocatalyst should be able to say "sustainable and environmentally friendly catalyst with Low cost ".

This review demonstrated the carbonaceous-TiO2 photocatalyst towards the enhanced H2 rate efficacy by water splitting process. In particular, the formation of an interface between the carbonaceous-TiO2 can provide efficient charge separation [221,222]. In addition to that, chemical bonding between them favors the narrowing TiO2 band gap and results in the improved light absorption [158,223]. Moreover, the literature reports suggested the noble metals such as Au, Pd, Ag, and Pt, play a central character in the additional improvement of H2 efficiency but the cost and availability make them challenging to access. Simple synthesis process also showed the strong intimate interaction between the carbonaceous-TiO2 photocatalysts [127,224–227]. So far, the review on carbonaceous materials for the better and clear understanding to control and design of the photocatalyst with the suitable mechanism of enhancing the H2 production activity are reserved [228–230].

Apart from the accomplishment of outstanding photocatalytic performance by carbonaceous-TiO2 based composites, H2 efficiency needs to be improved. Therefore, several strategies need to be developed to enhance the H2 production in photocatalysis. Besides, there is a need to replace expensive and precious metals like Pt with low cost and abundantly available materials. In the carbonaceous-TiO2 materials system, it is necessary to study extensively about CQDs and GQDs as they can be synthesized from lemon and orange juices in an eco-friendly manner which could be beneficial to other biological applications of photocatalysis in addition to hydrogen production. Moreover, deeper studies are required on the interface engineering between the carbonaceous-TiO2 nanocomposites and its beneficial properties for improved H2 production. Some more experimental studies should be required to study the transfer of electrons between carbon and TiO2 because still difficult to understand the electron flow process from the reported literature. Since there are no clear reports on how carbon materials transfer an electron into TiO2 or trap electron from TiO2. Therefore, it is recommended to that upcoming work should be intensive on more accurate approach on targeted adjustment to enhance their reusability and efficiency in the H2 production process. Future progress should be to understand and control of whether the nanocarbon acts as an electron donor to the TiO2, and therefore as the photoactive component, or as an acceptor from the TiO2. Current studies using carbon-based photocatalytic materials mostly focus on the photocatalytic H2 production in the presence of sacrificial agents. However, for the real application objective, the photocatalytic H2 production based on overall water splitting is in fact much closer to the concept of “clean and renewable energy”. Hence it is highly encouraged to popularize the investigation of carbon-based photocatalytic materials on photocatalytic overall water splitting.

This review highlighted the benefits of carbonaceous-TiO2 nanostructures in the photocatalytic water splitting. These carbonaceous-TiO2 materials found to show good carrier transport and electron accepting properties, which are the fundamental beneficial properties towards improving the hydrogen production efficiency through the reduced the photogenerated charge carries an enhanced light absorption towards the visible region. Moreover, the familiar interface interaction among the carbonaceous-TiO2 photocatalyst is favorable for the charge separation. In the carbonaceous-TiO2 photocatalysts, mostly three types of interfaces such as Schottky barrier (SB), surface plasmonic resonance (SPR), and the Z-scheme interface could be formed.

TiO2-graphene photocatalysts for the hydrogen production widely reported, which showed enhanced hydrogen evolution efficiency and showed excellent stability compared to other TiO2-based nanomaterials. It is observed that H2 production by photocatalysis process of TiO2 with graphene and CNTs integration demonstrated the effective separation, utilization charge carriers, and effective transfer of an electron between the TiO2 and surroundings. The other carbon materials are [carbon and activated carbon] widely investigated towards the removal of organic dyes via photocatalysis process due to the factor that activated carbon and carbon materials possess a high adsorption capacity. The recently developed carbonaceous materials such as 3-D graphene, CNHs, CNCs, and CQDs have also been studied for the practical photocatalytic application along with TiO2.
